questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose $n_p$ and $n_e$ represent the numbers of holes and conduction electrons, respectively, in an intrinsic semiconductor.
questions.solution:
In an intrinsic semiconductor, charge carriers are generated by the thermal excitation of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band.

Each excitation creates exactly one free electron in the conduction band and leaves behind exactly one hole in the valence band.

Therefore, the number of conduction electrons is always equal to the number of holes, which gives $n_p = n_e$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A semiconductor is subjected to an electric field. Suppose the number of charge carriers is $n$ and their average drift speed is $v$. Upon increasing the temperature,
questions.solution:
When the temperature of a semiconductor is increased, more covalent bonds are broken due to thermal energy. This leads to an increase in the number of electron-hole pairs, so the number of charge carriers $n$ increases.

The average drift speed of charge carriers is given by $v = \mu E$, where $\mu$ is the mobility and $E$ is the applied electric field. The mobility is given by $\mu = \dfrac{e \tau}{m}$, where $\tau$ is the relaxation time.

As temperature increases, the thermal agitation of lattice ions increases, causing charge carriers to collide more frequently. This decreases the relaxation time $\tau$.

Since $\tau$ decreases, the mobility $\mu$ decreases, which in turn causes the average drift speed $v$ to decrease.

Therefore, $n$ increases but $v$ decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electrical conduction within a semiconductor occurs due to
questions.solution:
In a semiconductor, electrical conduction takes place due to the movement of both electrons in the conduction band and holes in the valence band.

When an electric field is applied, electrons move in the direction opposite to the electric field, while holes move in the direction of the electric field, both contributing to the total current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Upon doping an intrinsic semiconductor with an impurity, its conductivity
questions.solution:
The conductivity of a semiconductor is given by $\sigma = e(n_e \mu_e + n_h \mu_h)$, where $n_e$ and $n_h$ are the number densities of electrons and holes respectively.

When an intrinsic semiconductor is doped with an impurity (either trivalent or pentavalent), the number of majority charge carriers (either holes or electrons) increases drastically.

This increase in the concentration of charge carriers results in a significant increase in the electrical conductivity of the semiconductor.

Thus, upon doping an intrinsic semiconductor with an impurity, its conductivity increases.

questions.subject: Physics
options: uncharged at $0 \text{ K}$ but charged at elevated temperatures
questions.solution:
A $p$-type semiconductor is formed by doping an intrinsic semiconductor with trivalent impurity atoms.

Since both the intrinsic semiconductor atoms and the impurity atoms are electrically neutral before doping, the resulting $p$-type semiconductor has an equal total number of protons and electrons.

Therefore, the semiconductor as a whole remains electrically neutral or uncharged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose $n_p$ and $n_e$ denote the numbers of holes and conduction electrons in an extrinsic semiconductor.
questions.solution:
In an intrinsic semiconductor, the number of holes is equal to the number of conduction electrons, giving $n_p = n_e$.

An extrinsic semiconductor is formed by doping an intrinsic semiconductor with either pentavalent or trivalent impurities.

For an n-type semiconductor, electrons are the majority charge carriers, so $n_e > n_p$.

For a p-type semiconductor, holes are the majority charge carriers, so $n_p > n_e$.

Therefore, in any extrinsic semiconductor, the number of holes is never equal to the number of conduction electrons, which implies $n_p \neq n_e$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a $p$-$n$ junction, the diffusion current is directed
options: from the $n$-side towards the $p$-side if forward-biased, and in the reverse direction if reverse-biased
from the $p$-side towards the $n$-side if forward-biased, and in the reverse direction if reverse-biased
questions.solution:
In a $p$-$n$ junction, the concentration of holes is higher in the $p$-region and the concentration of electrons is higher in the $n$-region.

Due to this concentration gradient, holes diffuse from the $p$-side to the $n$-side, and electrons diffuse from the $n$-side to the $p$-side.

The conventional current due to the flow of holes is in the direction of their motion (from $p$ to $n$), and the current due to the flow of electrons is opposite to their motion (also from $p$ to $n$).

Thus, the diffusion current is always directed from the $p$-side towards the $n$-side, irrespective of the biasing.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Within a $p$-$n$ junction, the drift current is directed
options: from the $n$-side towards the $p$-side when the junction is forward-biased, and in the reverse direction when it is reverse-biased
from the $p$-side towards the $n$-side when the junction is forward-biased, and in the reverse direction when it is reverse-biased
questions.solution:
In a $p$-$n$ junction, the depletion region contains uncovered positive donor ions on the $n$-side and negative acceptor ions on the $p$-side.

This charge distribution creates an internal electric field directed from the $n$-side towards the $p$-side.

The drift current is caused by the movement of minority charge carriers under the influence of this built-in electric field. Holes (minority carriers on the $n$-side) are swept towards the $p$-side, and electrons (minority carriers on the $p$-side) are swept towards the $n$-side.

The flow of positive charges from $n$ to $p$ and negative charges from $p$ to $n$ both contribute to a conventional current directed from the $n$-side to the $p$-side.

The direction of this drift current is independent of the external biasing.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a wire connects the two ends of a $p$-$n$ junction,
options: a steady current will exist from the $p$-side to the $n$-side
no steady current will exist in the circuit
a current might or might not exist depending on the resistance of the connecting wire
a steady current will exist from the $n$-side to the $p$-side
questions.solution:
When the two ends of a $p$-$n$ junction are connected by a wire, the circuit is closed but lacks any external source of electromotive force.

In thermal equilibrium, the drift current caused by the built-in electric field in the depletion region is exactly balanced by the diffusion current caused by the concentration gradient of charge carriers.

The built-in potential barrier cannot drive a steady current through the external wire. If a steady current were to flow, energy would be continuously dissipated as heat in the wire without any energy source, which violates the law of conservation of energy.

Therefore, the net current in the circuit remains zero and no steady current will exist.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a $p$-$n$ junction, the diffusion current exceeds the drift current in magnitude
questions.solution:
In an unbiased $p$-$n$ junction, the diffusion current and drift current are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, making the net current zero.

When the junction is reverse-biased, the width of the depletion region and the potential barrier increase. This opposes the diffusion of majority carriers, making the diffusion current negligible. The drift current, due to minority carriers, dominates.

When the junction is forward-biased, the applied voltage opposes the built-in potential, reducing the potential barrier. This allows a large number of majority carriers to diffuse across the junction. The drift current remains small and relatively unchanged. Thus, the diffusion current significantly exceeds the drift current in magnitude.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At $t = 0$, two identical capacitors $A$ and $B$, each initially charged to a potential $V$, are connected in two separate circuits as illustrated in the figure. The respective charges on the capacitors at time $t = CR$ are
questions.solution:
In circuit (a), the positive plate of capacitor A is connected to the p-side (anode) of the diode, and the negative plate is connected to the n-side (cathode) through the resistor. This means the diode is forward-biased and acts as a closed switch, allowing current to flow.

Capacitor A will discharge through the resistor R. The charge on the capacitor at any time $t$ during discharging is given by the equation:

$q_A(t) = q_0 e^{-\dfrac{t}{RC}}$

Given the initial charge $q_0 = CV$ and time $t = CR$, the charge on capacitor A is:

$q_A = CV e^{-\dfrac{CR}{CR}} = \dfrac{CV}{e}$

In circuit (b), the positive plate of capacitor B is connected to the n-side (cathode) of the diode, and the negative plate is connected to the p-side (anode) through the resistor. This means the diode is reverse-biased and acts as an open switch.

Since the circuit is open, no current can flow and capacitor B will not discharge. The charge on capacitor B remains constant at its initial value:

$q_B = CV$

Therefore, the respective charges on the capacitors at time $t = CR$ are $VC/e$ and $VC$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As illustrated in the figure, two identical $p$-$n$ junctions can be connected in series with a battery in three different ways. The potential difference across the two $p$-$n$ junctions is equal in
questions.solution:
In the given circuits, the longer line of the battery symbol represents the positive terminal and the shorter, thicker line represents the negative terminal. Thus, the left side of each circuit is connected to the positive terminal and the right side to the negative terminal.

Let us analyze the biasing of the $p$-$n$ junctions in each circuit:

In Circuit 1, the left $p$-$n$ junction has its $p$-side connected to the positive terminal, making it forward-biased. The right junction has its $p$-side connected to the negative terminal, making it reverse-biased. Since a reverse-biased junction offers much higher resistance than a forward-biased one, the potential drops across the two junctions will be unequal.

In Circuit 2, both $p$-$n$ junctions are oriented such that their $p$-sides are at a higher potential relative to their $n$-sides. Thus, both are forward-biased. Being identical and connected in series, they carry the same current and offer the same resistance, resulting in equal potential drops across them.

In Circuit 3, both $p$-$n$ junctions are oriented such that their $n$-sides are at a higher potential relative to their $p$-sides. Thus, both are reverse-biased. Being identical and connected in series, they offer the same resistance, resulting in equal potential drops across them.

Therefore, the potential difference across the two $p$-$n$ junctions is equal in Circuit 2 and Circuit 3.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a $p$-$n$ junction, a hole diffuses from the $p$-side to the $n$-side. This indicates that
options: a conduction electron on the $p$-side jumps to a broken bond to fill it
a bond breaks on the $p$-side and the electron released from the bond jumps to a broken bond on the $n$-side to fill it
a bond breaks on the $n$-side and the electron released from the bond jumps to a broken bond on the $p$-side to fill it
a bond breaks on the $n$-side and the electron released from the bond jumps to the conduction band
questions.solution:
A hole in a semiconductor is a vacancy in a covalent bond (valence band).

The movement of a hole from one position to another is physically realized by the movement of a valence electron in the opposite direction. When a hole diffuses from the $p$-side to the $n$-side, a valence electron from the $n$-side must move to the $p$-side to fill the vacancy.

This means a covalent bond breaks on the $n$-side, and the electron released from this bond jumps to fill a broken bond (hole) on the $p$-side. As a result, the vacancy (hole) effectively shifts from the $p$-side to the $n$-side.

questions.subject: Physics
options: all three regions have identical impurity concentrations
the emitter has the lowest impurity concentration
the collector has the lowest impurity concentration
the base has the lowest impurity concentration
questions.solution:
In a transistor, the three regions have different levels of doping.

The emitter is heavily doped to inject a large number of charge carriers into the base.

The base is very thin and lightly doped to minimize the recombination of charge carriers.

The collector is moderately doped.

Therefore, the base has the lowest impurity concentration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Consider the following incomplete sentence regarding transistors:
The emitter–…… junction is ___ and the collector– …… junction is ___.
The proper words to fill the dotted empty spaces are, respectively,
questions.solution:
A transistor consists of three regions: emitter, base, and collector.

The two junctions in a bipolar junction transistor are formed between the emitter and the base, and between the collector and the base. These are known as the emitter-base junction and the collector-base junction.

For normal active operation of a transistor, the emitter-base junction is forward biased and the collector-base junction is reverse biased.

Therefore, the proper words to fill the dotted empty spaces are 'base' and 'base', respectively.
